brief_document.title: Engine seam and time/signal/query/child/concurrency module scaffolding
brief_document.purpose: Establish the narrow engine-facing seam this cluster consumes (residency resolution, mailbox delivery, child-spawn request, timer-wheel arm/disarm, and recording through the target workflow's single Recorder) and the module skeleton for timers, signals, queries, child workflows, and concurrency. Every later brief builds against this seam; defining it first keeps the AE boundary explicit and lets the whole cluster be tested with a fake engine handle. The crate scaffold (lib.rs) is created by AE-001, which must land first; this brief adds its module lines to it.
brief_document.task: Define an engine-facing trait (the seam) inside the aion crate that exposes exactly five capabilities: resolve a WorkflowId to a live process handle (or report non-resident/terminal/unknown), deliver a message to a workflow process mailbox, request AE to spawn a child workflow execution as a linked process, arm/disarm a beamr timer-wheel entry for a given fire time, and record an event through the target workflow's single Recorder (so AT's asynchronous-arrival events go through the one per-workflow writer, AD CO6 — never EventStore::append directly). Provide the module declarations (mod.rs files containing only pub mod + pub use) for the time, signal, query, child, and concurrency modules. Provide an in-test fake implementation of the seam so subsequent briefs can test without AE. This brief defines no timer/signal/query/child behaviour itself.

requirements.title: EngineHandle seam trait
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define an EngineHandle trait exposing residency resolution, mailbox delivery, child-spawn request, timer-wheel arm/disarm, and recording through the target workflow's single Recorder. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT expose workflow lifecycle, supervision, or module-loading operations on this trait.
acceptance: engine_seam.rs defines a trait (e.g. EngineHandle) with methods to: resolve a WorkflowId to a process-handle result that distinguishes Resident(handle) / NonResident / Terminal / Unknown; deliver a message to a workflow process mailbox by handle; request a child workflow spawn (input: parent WorkflowId + child workflow_type + input Payload + RunId, returns the child's process handle + WorkflowId) linked to the parent; arm a timer-wheel entry (workflow process + TimerId + fire_at) and disarm one (workflow process + TimerId); and record an aion-core Event for a workflow through that workflow's single Recorder (the asynchronous-arrival append path), returning a typed error on failure.
The record-through-Recorder method is how AT's TimerFired / SignalReceived / ChildWorkflow* appends reach the single per-workflow writer (AD CO6); AT services never call EventStore::append directly.
The trait is Send + Sync so services holding it are usable across scheduler threads.

The trait carries no method that starts, supervises, or tears down a top-level workflow process — only the five seam capabilities (the residency-resolution, child-spawn, and record methods are requests into AE, not lifecycle ownership here).
Doc comments state that AE implements this trait and that this cluster never manages process lifecycle directly (CO6).

requirements.title: Module scaffolding for time/signal/query/child/concurrency
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL create the time, signal, query, child, and concurrency module trees with mod.rs files. Each mod.rs SHALL contain only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports.
acceptance: crates/aion/src/time/mod.rs, signal/mod.rs, query/mod.rs, child/mod.rs, concurrency/mod.rs exist and contain only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports (no functions, types, traits) per CO3.
lib.rs declares the engine_seam, time, signal, query, child, and concurrency modules and re-exports the EngineHandle trait.

cargo check -p aion passes with the empty module files in place (placeholder named files may be declared as they are created by later briefs; an empty mod.rs that compiles is acceptable for modules whose contents land later).

requirements.title: Fake engine handle for tests
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a test-only fake implementation of EngineHandle so this cluster's tests can exercise delivery, residency, child spawn, and wheel arm/disarm without AE.
acceptance: A fake EngineHandle (behind cfg(test) or a test-support module) records delivered mailbox messages, configurable residency answers per WorkflowId, requested child spawns, armed/disarmed wheel entries, and events appended through the record-through-Recorder method (so tests can assert AT recorded the right events), so tests can assert on them.
The fake lets a test set a WorkflowId to Resident / NonResident / Terminal / Unknown and observe how a service reacts.
The fake lets a test drive a wheel-entry fire (invoke the callback/path a real wheel would) so timer-firing logic is testable without real time passing.
A unit test constructs the fake, marks a workflow Resident, delivers a message, and asserts the message was captured.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement timer, signal, query, child, or concurrency behaviour — those land in AT-002 through AT-012.
SHALL NOT implement the real AE-side of the seam — AE owns that; this brief defines the trait and a test fake only.
SHALL NOT add HTTP/gRPC/WS transport — that is AW.
verification: All mod.rs files contain only declarations and re-exports (CO3).
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: An engine-facing handle/trait (consumed, not owned, by this cluster) exposes: resolve a WorkflowId to a live process handle, deliver a message to a workflow process mailbox, request AE to spawn a child workflow, and arm/disarm a beamr timer-wheel entry.
The engine seam is an explicit trait (or handle type) so this cluster never manages workflow process lifecycle, supervision, or module loading directly.
The time/signal/query/child/concurrency modules live under the aion crate with mod.rs files containing only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports.

stories.text: As the engine, I want every timer firing, signal arrival, and child outcome recorded as an aion-core Event so that replay can return the same observation without re-waiting or re-spawning.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny across the cluster's modules.
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
Library errors use thiserror domain types; no anyhow in library code; no unwrap/expect in library code; lock poisoning handled explicitly.

These modules live in the aion crate. The seam with AE (workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, residency resolution) is via an explicit engine-facing handle/trait this cluster consumes; this cluster does not manage process lifecycle.

Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed is recorded as an aion-core Event before/at the point of delivery; queries record nothing. No code path delivers a recordable observation without recording it (no silent observation).

Durable timers go through EventStore::schedule_timer / expired_timers; all AT event appends (TimerStarted/Fired/Cancelled, SignalReceived, ChildWorkflow*) go through the target workflow's single Recorder via the engine seam (AD CO6) — never a direct EventStore::append and no bespoke side-channel persistence outside the aion-store contract.

Tests run against aion-store's InMemoryStore; timer expiry, signal delivery + durability, query reply, child spawn, and all/race/map each have explicit test coverage. Time-dependent tests inject the clock — no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
Timer poll intervals, query timeouts, and recovery-tick cadence are engine-configured, never hardcoded defaults (per the no-assumed-defaults rule).

resolved_context.intention: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow spawning, and the all/race/map concurrency primitives. A workflow process sleeps for a month and wakes; an external system signals an awaiting workflow and the workflow continues in microseconds; an operator queries a live workflow without perturbing it; a parent spawns children and fans work out across the BEAM. These are the interaction surfaces, and they must be both microsecond-fast on the live path and exactly recoverable after a crash. Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed (a timer firing, a signal arriving, a child completing) is recorded as an aion-core Event so that AD's replay returns the same observation without re-waiting; every interaction that observes without changing (a query) is never recorded. The work here is the live half of durable execution — recording the observable, delivering it over beamr's native mailbox and timer wheel, and recovering it on restart — paired with AD's replay half that reads those same events back.

provenance.quote: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow
